chapters.title: Is sleep already a memory?
chapters.text:
Sleep loss tops the list of hard parts about becoming a new parent. Instead of savoring those first months with the baby, you shuffle through a haze of yawns and self-doubt, second-guessing every move and wondering whether you are getting it wrong.

Of course, every baby cries at night, every baby wakes at night, and most babies do not sleep through the night on a regular basis. Be realistic about that, but stay open to the idea that small changes can lift the fog and bring back some of your own health and well-being.

All babies wake several times each night, and that has nothing to do with your parenting skill.

The No-Cry Sleep Solution walks through ten steps that let you craft a sleep plan tailored to your baby. Elizabeth Pantley holds a strong belief that sleep should feel calm and pleasant, even if it is necessary, and not something a child learns to dread. A personalized plan plants that mindset from the start.

Pantley lived through the same struggle. A mother of four, she chased answers to the sleep challenge with every child. The two routes she kept hearing about were letting the baby cry it out or being there on demand all night long. She disliked both, so she kept looking.

Raising four unique children has shown her that, while a very young baby can sleep through the night, it is the exception rather than the rule.

Elizabeth Pantley

Pantley recruited a group of test mothers to try the ideas in the book. The results were encouraging. By day ten, 42 percent of the babies were sleeping through the night. By day sixty, 92 percent had reached the same point. Still, the process takes time, so plan to stick with it.

On average, a new parent loses about 109 minutes of sleep each night during the first year with a newborn.

chapters.title: Ten steps toward a steadier sleep rhythm
chapters.text:
Several pieces of the plan involve repetition and review, since there is no single template that fits every baby, and you have to try things, watch what happens, and adjust as you go.

Your baby is one of a kind, and no universal fix exists for sleep trouble. The path runs through trial and error.

The book's ten-step path works like this. Begin by running through a safety sweep of the sleep setup. Then learn the core facts about how sleep works. Next, write down your baby's current sleep habits. After that, study the different approaches and pick the ones worth trying. Draft a plan and follow it for ten days. Keep a log across those ten days and look for patterns. Take stock of what worked and adjust if you need to. Run the new plan for another ten days. Wrap up with a final review and any last tweaks.

At the end of every ten-day stretch, go back over your log and decide whether fresh changes are in order. Roll those changes in and give them another ten days. One or two nights is not enough to know if a change is paying off, but after ten days a new rhythm has had time to settle in, and you will know if it fits.

Hold any tweak to the sleep routine for a full ten days before you decide it is time for another change.

chapters.title: Your baby's safety is the top priority
chapters.text:
Your baby's safety is the most important thing, and a few safety rules deserve your attention when it comes to sleep. Talk to your doctor if anything feels unclear or if you are worried about how you lay the baby down.

SIDS, or Sudden Infant Death Syndrome, is a real worry for every parent. It is the unexplained death of a child under one year old, and it usually happens during sleep. Most of the time, the child was healthy. Researchers have run many studies, and they still cannot point to a clear cause, so the work is to lower the risk where you can.

The cause of SIDS is unknown, so the work is to lower the risk and stay safe.

Babies often prefer to sleep on their tummies, but that position is not considered safe. Putting the baby to sleep on their back is widely thought to be far safer, and it is worth encouraging as much as you can. Daytime naps in a stroller, infant seat, or car seat all keep them on their back. Once the baby is awake and alert, give them tummy time to help the major muscle groups develop.

Tummy sleeping is not considered safe, so lay the baby down on their back.

Run a quick safety sweep of the sleep area. Make sure everything in the crib meets safety standards, and that the mattress fits tight against the crib with no gaps. Skip stuffed toys and pillows in the crib. Avoid blankets over or under the baby, and make sure the sleep clothes fit well and are not baggy.

All gear in the sleep area should meet strict safety standards.

Make sure there is a working baby monitor in the room, and keep lamps and nightlights away from the bed. A comfortable sleep temperature usually sits between 64 and 72 degrees Fahrenheit.

Pantley knows that many parents like to co-sleep, but the general safety advice is to skip it.

No matter how tired you are, no matter how tempting the moment looks, please put your baby's safety above everything else.

Elizabeth Pantley

Each year, more than 2000 babies in the United States die of SIDS, so a safe sleep setup is a must.

chapters.title: Knowing the basics of sleep helps you tackle the problem
chapters.text:
You cannot fix a problem if you do not understand the background. Knowing why we sleep and how the body regulates it helps you figure out how much rest your baby actually needs, and how to handle the rough nights.

Adults run on a biological clock, often called the circadian rhythm, that controls when we feel sleepy and when we feel awake. It is what lets us tell day from night, and it has natural peaks and dips. Travel and jet lag can throw it off for a while, but it usually resets. Babies are not born with a working circadian rhythm. It has to develop over time.

The circadian rhythm controls when you sleep and wake, and it is sometimes called your body clock. A baby is not born with this rhythm, and it has to develop.

In the early months, your rhythm is out of sync with the baby's sleep.

Over time, the baby's naps and night sleep start to build a pattern, but the rhythm usually does not mature until around six to nine months of age, and it does not really kick in until around four to five months.

A baby's circadian rhythm is usually not set until around nine months of age, at the earliest.

Every baby is different, but a typical sleep cycle looks like this. The baby gets tired and drifts off. A stretch of light sleep follows. Then a deep sleep for about an hour. A brief wake-up. Another deep sleep for one to two hours. Another brief wake-up. REM sleep, the dreaming stage. Another brief wake-up. Light sleep. Another brief wake-up. REM sleep. Another brief wake-up. Light sleep. Then a longer stretch of being awake.

The trouble is in those short wake-ups. Those small moments can stretch into long periods of awake time if the sleep area is off or if you do not answer what the baby needs quickly. On top of that, you may struggle to fall back asleep after the baby's brief wake-up, so you drift off just as they wake again.

REM sleep, the dreaming stage, plays a big part in brain development.

chapters.title: Why sleep cues matter
chapters.text:
Pantley points out that the best way to help a baby sleep is to set up sleep cues. Those are the actions you take and the environment you build that help your baby recognize when it is time to sleep.

William C. Dement, a doctor and researcher, is considered the world's leading expert on sleep, having set up the world's first center for sleep disorders at Stanford University. He believes sleep cues are the single best way to improve a child's sleep and to settle them into a routine they will keep.

Sleep cues help your child recognize when it is time to sleep.

Between the ages of five months and five years, the cues you set become the single biggest factor in your child's sleep patterns. Pay attention to what your child does before sleep, the time you put them down, the environment they are in, and how relaxed or wound up they are. That helps them draw the line that says it is time to sleep.

Putting your newborn down for sleep in a way that meets their needs raises the chance that they will fall asleep quickly and stay asleep until morning. When we say a baby slept through the night, we mean they slept for about five hours. Most adults would not call five hours a full night, and sleeping that long as a newborn is rare.

For a newborn, sleeping through the night usually means a stretch of five hours, and that is rare.

A newborn has a small stomach, and they are only drinking liquids, so the food digests fast. You will need to feed your baby every two to four hours, and some babies need more. Some babies fall asleep while feeding, but do not let that become the default. If you do, the baby will start to believe that the only way to fall asleep is by feeding at the breast, the bottle, or a pacifier.

Pantley suggests that, when you see your baby getting sleepy, move the feeding aid away and wait. If they fuss for more, move it back and then move it away again. Repeat until they fall asleep. Over time, they will learn to fall asleep on their own, without needing to suck.

Help your baby understand that they can fall asleep without feeding by moving the bottle or breast away before they drift off.

It is tempting to let the baby fall asleep in your arms. Your child will do that every time if you let them, because they feel safe and warm in your arms, and they will start to link sleep with your arms. When the baby starts to look sleepy, put them down in the crib instead. If you skip that step, the baby will cry until you pick them up, which is a habit you do not want.

Fisted hands can be a sign of hunger in many babies, while relaxed hands after or during feeding usually mean they are full.

chapters.title: Watch for your baby's unique sleep signals
chapters.text:
Your baby will give you signals that they are sleepy, but you have to spot them at the right moment. Be realistic. Your baby is not going to start sleeping for long stretches at night right away, but if you stay with it, you will encourage longer stretches and a clear pattern.

When you see sleepy cues, put the baby down. Common cues include slowing movements, less interest in toys or people, glazing over, a little fussiness, eye rubbing, or yawning.

Sleepy cues include slowing movements, glazing over, eye rubbing, yawning, or losing interest in toys.

When the baby wakes at night, listen to the noises they make. You will start to learn your baby's unique sounds. Some sucking or sniffling sounds may mean the baby is still asleep, and in that case, let them sleep. If you pick up signs that the baby is about to wake fully, tend to their needs quickly. That helps them stay sleepy and drop back off far easier than if you let them wake up all the way.

If the baby looks ready to wake in the night, tend to them quickly so they can fall back asleep with less fuss.

For naps, do not let a newborn nap for too long, or it can throw off the night sleep and leave you in for a long, broken night.

Do not let a newborn nap for too long, or they will mix up day and night. Aim for short daytime naps and long nighttime sleep.

Naps that are too close to bedtime can throw off nighttime sleep. For a newborn, early evening is about as late as a nap should run.

chapters.title: Your baby is unique, and that is the point
chapters.text:
Around four months, your child's sleep pattern starts to shift. Treat your child as a unique person in their own right, and expect their sleep pattern to look different from your friend's child. Ask yourself whether the sleep pattern is a real problem or whether it just feels like one because you are comparing them to someone else.

Comparing your child's sleep to another child's is not useful, because every child is unique.

Your child's sleep is only a problem if it is not enough or if it is messing with your own life and health.

A lot of parents listen too closely to what other people say, and that leads them to think there is a real problem when there is not. Every child is unique, and just because a friend's child sleeps for a certain number of hours in the afternoon and then sleeps most of the night does not mean there is anything wrong with your child if they do not follow the same pattern.

Pantley points out that her own four children were all completely different, and that means you cannot compare one child to another in the same family either.

chapters.title: Keep the bedtime routine steady and predictable
chapters.text:
What you do in the hour before bed shapes the rhythm and sends a clear signal that sleep is on the way.

Start with the basics. Make sure the baby is not hungry, and look at what they eat before bed. Some foods are easier on little stomachs than others, and a heavy meal can break up the night. Sugar before bed is a bad idea. A few good options in the two hours before sleep include whole grain cereal, brown rice, yogurt, cheese, fruit, and oatmeal, as long as your child is eating solids.

Check the pre-bed food. Make sure the baby is fed but is not eating anything that will keep them wired.

Make sure the sleep space feels right, and dress the baby for the weather. They should not be too warm and not too cold.

Once those basics are in place, work on a bedtime flow that helps them wind down. Helpful cues include a warm bath, a gentle massage, a story, slow music, rocking, a feed, and even a short walk. The hour before bed should be calm, quiet, and softly lit.

A steady bedtime flow trains the baby's mind to expect sleep.

The flow has to stay the same every single night. With time, it becomes second nature and the baby takes the cue that sleep is near. The same goes for naps. Keep nap times on a fixed clock and follow the same mini-routine beforehand. That habit can lower your own stress and free up a little time for you.

A lot of parents who skip a formal bedtime flow end up spending the last hour of the day arguing with their child about going to bed.

Elizabeth Pantley

Cherries carry a small amount of melatonin, the hormone that helps the body wind down. A few cherries before bed can be useful if the child is old enough for them.

chapters.title: So what time is bedtime?
chapters.text:
A lot of parents are not sure what time their child's bedtime should be. Every child is different, and the answer depends on you, but most children are ready for sleep around 6:30 to 7:00 in the evening.

Most children are ready for sleep by 7 PM.

Some parents are tempted to push bedtime a little later, hoping the child will sleep longer. That move usually backfires. The child gets overtired, and getting them down becomes harder. Overtired children can also end up sleep-deprived.

Putting your child to bed a little earlier is fine, even if you worry about early wake-ups, because most babies sleep longer with an earlier bedtime. A steady daytime rhythm, for example feeding at the same time each day, playtime at the same time, and a walk at the same time, helps the baby's internal clock settle in, and sleep becomes easier to manage in a shorter window.

A steady daytime rhythm, doing the same things in the same order, helps the baby's biological clock take hold.

The baby should take regular naps through the day, but do not let them nap too long, and do not let the last nap run too close to bedtime.

The number of naps and the length of each one depend on age, and the numbers below are just a starting point, because every child is different. On average, a four-month-old should have three naps a day, with a total nap time of four to six hours.

Every young child needs naps, but the number and length depend on age.

As the child grows, the need for naps drops. An average nine-month-old should have two naps a day, totaling about two and a half to four hours.

Napping too late in the day is a recipe for broken sleep at night, and it can also make it harder to get the baby down. If the baby is at the three-nap stage, good times are mid-morning, early afternoon, and early evening. If the baby is at two naps, mid-morning and early afternoon work well. If the baby is down to one nap, aim for early afternoon. When the baby starts to show tired signs through their own cues, put them down for a nap.

Do not let the baby nap too close to bedtime, or nighttime sleep will suffer.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Reading your child's sleep cues and laying out a steady rhythm are both key to building a sleep pattern that works for you and your child. Make changes slowly, and give each one a full ten days to see if it works before you try anything else.

Elizabeth Pantley encourages you to log everything related to your baby's sleep. Logging helps you spot where changes are needed. Make one change at a time, or you risk confusing the baby and making the sleep situation worse.

Most new parents say goodbye to good sleep for a few months after the baby arrives, but it does not have to last. You will get your sleep back, and if you follow the advice in The No-Cry Sleep Solution, that day may arrive sooner rather than later.

Try this. Keep a log of your baby's sleep patterns, including the environment when they sleep and wake up, like noise, light, and so on. Think carefully about your child's bedtime, and set a standard time that is not too early and not too late. Plan out your new pre-bedtime routine before you set the wheels in motion.

Read it till the end?
